question: A startup wants to run a web application without managing any servers, operating systems, or runtime environments. Which cloud service model best fits this requirement?
choices: A. IaaS — the provider manages hardware; the startup manages everything else
B. PaaS — the provider manages the runtime and infrastructure; the startup manages only the application and data
C. SaaS — the provider manages the entire stack including the application logic
D. FaaS — the provider manages individual function invocations only
correct: B. PaaS — the provider manages the runtime and infrastructure; the startup manages only the application and data
explanation: PaaS (Platform as a Service) abstracts away OS, middleware, and runtime management, leaving the customer responsible only for deploying and managing their application code and data. IaaS (A) still requires the customer to manage the OS and everything above it. SaaS (C) provides a finished application with no customer code involved. FaaS/serverless (D) is a subset of PaaS but the question asks about running a web application with a persistent runtime, which maps to PaaS. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cloud Concepts.
topic: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service model differences and when to use each

question: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, which of the following is ALWAYS the customer's responsibility regardless of which AWS service is used?
choices: A. Patching the underlying hypervisor
B. Managing physical data center access controls
C. Classifying data and controlling who has access to it
D. Replacing failed hard drives in the storage layer
correct: C. Classifying data and controlling who has access to it
explanation: AWS's shared responsibility model divides security into 'security of the cloud' (AWS's domain) and 'security in the cloud' (the customer's domain). Data classification and access control — deciding what data is sensitive and who may read or write it — always belongs to the customer regardless of whether they use EC2, S3, Lambda, or a managed service. Hypervisor patching (A), physical data center controls (B), and hardware replacement (D) are all AWS responsibilities. Source: https://aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model/
topic: Shared responsibility model: what the cloud provider manages versus what the customer manages

question: An application deployed in a single AWS Availability Zone experiences downtime whenever the AZ has a power event. What architectural change directly addresses this?
choices: A. Move the application to a different AWS Region
B. Deploy identical application instances across at least two AZs within the same Region
C. Enable AWS Shield to protect against infrastructure failures
D. Switch from EC2 to Lambda to eliminate AZ dependencies
correct: B. Deploy identical application instances across at least two AZs within the same Region
explanation: Availability Zones are physically separate data centers within a Region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking. Spreading workloads across multiple AZs means a failure in one AZ does not take down the application — the load balancer routes traffic to healthy AZs. Moving to a different Region (A) migrates the problem rather than solving it and adds latency. AWS Shield (C) protects against DDoS, not infrastructure failures. Lambda (D) runs across AZs by default but is not the direct fix for an EC2 architecture; the root cause is single-AZ placement. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar.
topic: Regions vs Availability Zones and why multi-AZ deployments matter for reliability

question: A team needs to isolate their production workloads from their development workloads at the network level in AWS. Which construct provides this isolation by default?
choices: A. An IAM boundary policy applied to the production account
B. A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) with its own private IP address space
C. An S3 bucket policy restricting cross-environment access
D. A CloudTrail trail scoped to the production environment
correct: B. A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) with its own private IP address space
explanation: A VPC is a logically isolated virtual network in AWS. Resources inside a VPC cannot communicate with resources in another VPC unless explicitly configured (via peering, Transit Gateway, etc.). Placing production and development in separate VPCs — or separate accounts each with their own VPCs — enforces network-level isolation by default. IAM boundary policies (A) restrict identity permissions, not network routing. S3 bucket policies (C) control object access, not network-layer isolation. CloudTrail (D) provides audit logging, not isolation. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Security Pillar (Network Protection).
topic: VPC concepts: isolated virtual networks and network-level segmentation in the cloud

question: A company runs a batch processing job that can tolerate interruptions and needs the lowest possible compute cost. Which EC2 pricing model is most appropriate?
choices: A. On-Demand instances, because they provide guaranteed availability
B. Reserved instances with a 1-year commitment, because they are cheaper than On-Demand
C. Spot instances, because AWS can reclaim them with 2-minute notice and they offer up to 90% discount
D. Dedicated Hosts, because they isolate the workload on physical hardware
correct: C. Spot instances, because AWS can reclaim them with 2-minute notice and they offer up to 90% discount
explanation: Spot instances use spare AWS capacity and can be interrupted with a 2-minute warning, making them ideal for fault-tolerant, interruptible batch jobs at the lowest cost. On-Demand (A) guarantees availability but is the most expensive per-hour option. Reserved instances (B) are cheaper than On-Demand for steady, predictable workloads but require upfront commitment and don't help an interruptible job. Dedicated Hosts (D) provide physical isolation for compliance needs and are the most expensive option. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cost Optimization Pillar.
topic: Cloud pricing models: on-demand, reserved, and spot/preemptible instances and when to use each

question: Which statement most accurately distinguishes PaaS from SaaS from a developer's operational perspective?
choices: A. PaaS provides a finished application; SaaS provides a deployment platform
B. PaaS requires managing the OS; SaaS does not
C. PaaS lets developers deploy custom code; SaaS delivers ready-to-use software with no custom code deployment
D. PaaS and SaaS are identical; the distinction is only marketing
correct: C. PaaS lets developers deploy custom code; SaaS delivers ready-to-use software with no custom code deployment
explanation: The key operational distinction is: with PaaS (e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine), the developer writes and deploys their own application code on a managed platform. With SaaS (e.g., Salesforce, Gmail), the entire application is delivered by the provider and the customer only configures or uses it — no code deployment happens. Option A reverses the definitions. Option B mischaracterizes PaaS (the OS is managed by the provider). Option D is incorrect; the distinction has significant operational implications. Source: NIST SP 800-145 cloud service model definitions.
topic: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service model differences and when to use each

question: A financial services firm must keep all customer data within a specific country due to regulatory requirements. Which cloud concept directly enables this?
choices: A. Selecting a specific AWS Availability Zone within a Region
B. Selecting a cloud Region located in that country and using services that do not replicate outside it
C. Enabling AWS Shield Advanced to enforce data sovereignty
D. Encrypting data with a customer-managed KMS key
correct: B. Selecting a cloud Region located in that country and using services that do not replicate outside it
explanation: Cloud Regions are geographically distinct clusters of data centers. AWS guarantees that data stored in a Region is not moved outside that Region without explicit customer action. To meet data residency requirements, the team must choose a Region in the required country AND verify that the specific services used do not silently replicate data cross-region. Selecting an AZ (A) is a subset of a Region, not a geographic boundary. AWS Shield (C) is a DDoS protection service unrelated to data residency. Encryption (D) protects confidentiality but does not prevent data from being stored or replicated in other countries. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Security Pillar.
topic: Regions vs Availability Zones and geographic data residency considerations

question: A company is evaluating whether to move its on-premises data warehouse to the cloud. Which trade-off most accurately represents the cloud's disadvantage in this scenario?
choices: A. Cloud data warehouses cannot be scaled horizontally
B. The company loses direct control over hardware procurement and physical security
C. Cloud providers do not offer managed data warehouse services
D. On-premises warehouses are always cheaper for large datasets
correct: B. The company loses direct control over hardware procurement and physical security
explanation: Moving to the cloud means the provider owns and manages the physical infrastructure, which is a genuine trade-off: the company surrenders physical security oversight and hardware choice in exchange for elasticity, reduced operational burden, and pay-as-you-go pricing. A is false — cloud data warehouses (e.g., Redshift, BigQuery) scale horizontally as a core feature. C is false — AWS Redshift, Google BigQuery, and Azure Synapse are all managed cloud data warehouse services. D is not universally true; total cost of ownership depends heavily on workload patterns, and many organizations save money in the cloud. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cost Optimization Pillar.
topic: Cloud vs on-premises trade-offs: elasticity, cost, control, and operational burden

question: What is the primary purpose of an AWS Reserved Instance compared to an On-Demand instance?
choices: A. Reserved instances guarantee a specific physical host, preventing noisy-neighbor issues
B. Reserved instances provide a billing discount in exchange for a 1- or 3-year capacity commitment
C. Reserved instances are immune to spot-market interruptions
D. Reserved instances offer faster network throughput than On-Demand instances
correct: B. Reserved instances provide a billing discount in exchange for a 1- or 3-year capacity commitment
explanation: Reserved Instances are a billing construct, not a different type of hardware. Committing to use a specific instance type in a Region for 1 or 3 years earns discounts of 30–72% over On-Demand rates. They do not guarantee a physical host — that is what Dedicated Hosts do (A). Reserved instances are a different purchasing model from Spot instances, which are interruptible; RIs are not immune to spot interruptions because they are not spot instances (C). Network throughput is the same (D). Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cost Optimization Pillar.
topic: Cloud pricing models: on-demand, reserved, and spot/preemptible instances

question: A team's application is deployed in a single AWS Region and requires 99.99% availability. Which architectural pattern is necessary to meet this SLA?
choices: A. Deploy all application tiers in a single large EC2 instance with maximum resources
B. Distribute application tiers across at least three Availability Zones with load balancing and auto-scaling
C. Enable Cross-Region Replication for all S3 buckets
D. Use AWS Shield Advanced to protect against availability threats
correct: B. Distribute application tiers across at least three Availability Zones with load balancing and auto-scaling
explanation: 99.99% availability (roughly 52 minutes of downtime per year) within a single Region requires redundancy across multiple AZs, a load balancer to route traffic away from failed instances, and auto-scaling to replace unhealthy instances. A single large instance (A) is a single point of failure. Cross-Region S3 replication (C) addresses data durability, not compute availability. AWS Shield (D) defends against DDoS attacks but does not compensate for hardware or AZ failures. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar.
topic: Regions vs Availability Zones and multi-AZ deployment for high availability

question: A DevOps engineer needs to run a compute-intensive machine learning training job that requires GPUs. Which EC2 instance family should they select?
choices: A. T-series (T3, T4g) — burstable general-purpose instances
B. P-series or G-series — GPU-optimized instances designed for ML training and inference
C. R-series — memory-optimized instances with high RAM-to-CPU ratios
D. C-series — compute-optimized instances with high CPU-to-memory ratios
correct: B. P-series or G-series — GPU-optimized instances designed for ML training and inference
explanation: AWS P-series (P3, P4) and G-series (G4, G5) instances include NVIDIA GPUs specifically for machine learning training, inference, and graphics workloads. T-series (A) are burstable instances for variable-load general workloads that do not have GPUs. R-series (C) are memory-optimized for in-memory databases and caches. C-series (D) are CPU-optimized for HPC and batch processing but contain no GPUs. Instance family selection is one of the most common EC2 right-sizing decisions. Source: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/
topic: EC2 instance types and use cases for compute, memory, GPU, and burstable workloads

question: An application stores user-uploaded images that are accessed frequently for the first 30 days and then rarely thereafter. Which S3 storage configuration minimizes cost while maintaining access?
choices: A. Store everything in S3 Standard with no lifecycle policy
B. Store in S3 Standard; use a lifecycle policy to transition objects to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days
C. Store in S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval from the start to maximize savings
D. Store in S3 One Zone-IA to reduce cost by replicating to only one AZ
correct: B. Store in S3 Standard; use a lifecycle policy to transition objects to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days
explanation: S3 Standard is optimal for frequently accessed data. After 30 days, transitioning to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (Standard-IA) retains multi-AZ durability and millisecond retrieval while lowering storage cost by roughly 40%. Leaving everything in S3 Standard (A) wastes money on cold data. S3 Glacier (C) imposes retrieval fees and latency unsuitable for a user-facing application that still needs occasional access. S3 One Zone-IA (D) reduces durability by storing data in a single AZ, which is inappropriate for production user data. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/storage-class-intro.html
topic: S3 storage classes and lifecycle policies for cost-effective data management

question: A third-party partner needs temporary read-only access to a specific S3 bucket without creating an IAM user for them. Which mechanism is most appropriate?
choices: A. Share the root account access keys with a 24-hour expiry note
B. Generate a pre-signed URL with a defined expiration time that grants temporary access to the specific object or prefix
C. Make the S3 bucket publicly readable and inform the partner of the URL
D. Create a new IAM user, attach a read-only policy, and delete the user after access is no longer needed
correct: B. Generate a pre-signed URL with a defined expiration time that grants temporary access to the specific object or prefix
explanation: Pre-signed URLs embed temporary, cryptographically signed credentials that expire at a defined time. No IAM user is created; the URL itself grants access only to the specific resource for the specified duration. Sharing root credentials (A) violates the principle of least privilege and root credential best practices. Making the bucket public (C) exposes data to the entire internet. Creating and deleting an IAM user (D) is operationally cumbersome, risks forgetting to delete the user, and grants broader programmatic access than a pre-signed URL. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/ShareObjectPreSignedURL.html
topic: S3 pre-signed URLs for temporary, scoped access without IAM user creation

question: A Lambda function needs to read secrets from AWS Secrets Manager and write logs to CloudWatch. What is the most secure way to grant these permissions?
choices: A. Hardcode the AWS access key and secret key into the Lambda environment variables
B. Attach an IAM role to the Lambda function with a least-privilege policy granting access to Secrets Manager and CloudWatch Logs
C. Create an IAM user with full admin permissions and pass its credentials to the Lambda via environment variables
D. Store the access key in an S3 bucket and have the Lambda download it at startup
correct: B. Attach an IAM role to the Lambda function with a least-privilege policy granting access to Secrets Manager and CloudWatch Logs
explanation: IAM roles are the canonical way to grant AWS service permissions to other AWS services. Lambda automatically assumes the attached role and receives temporary, automatically rotated credentials — no long-lived secrets are stored anywhere. Hardcoding credentials (A) or storing them in environment variables (A, C) creates a static secret that can be leaked and is not auto-rotated. Creating an admin IAM user (C) violates least-privilege and creates a long-lived credential. Storing credentials in S3 (D) is an anti-pattern that adds latency and creates another secret management problem. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html
topic: IAM roles vs users vs policies and least-privilege access for AWS services

question: A team is deciding between Amazon RDS (PostgreSQL) and Amazon DynamoDB for storing user session data with millions of reads per second and a highly variable, schema-less structure. Which choice is more appropriate and why?
choices: A. RDS, because PostgreSQL supports ACID transactions and is always more reliable
B. DynamoDB, because it is a serverless, schema-flexible key-value/document store that scales horizontally to millions of requests per second
C. RDS, because relational databases always outperform NoSQL at high read volumes
D. DynamoDB, because it automatically migrates relational schemas to a document model
correct: B. DynamoDB, because it is a serverless, schema-flexible key-value/document store that scales horizontally to millions of requests per second
explanation: DynamoDB is purpose-built for high-throughput, low-latency access to schema-flexible data — exactly what session storage requires. It scales horizontally without the customer managing capacity, making it serverless in operational terms. RDS (A, C) is a managed relational database best suited for structured, relational data with complex queries and joins; it requires instance sizing and does not scale horizontally as easily. DynamoDB does not migrate relational schemas (D) — it requires designing a new access pattern-based data model. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Introduction.html
topic: RDS vs DynamoDB: relational managed databases vs NoSQL serverless data stores

question: An EC2 instance's CPU utilization has been above 90% for the past 15 minutes, but the on-call engineer was not alerted. Which AWS service and configuration is missing?
choices: A. AWS Config rule watching for CPU anomalies
B. A CloudWatch alarm on the CPUUtilization metric with an SNS action that notifies the on-call channel
C. AWS Trusted Advisor check for high-CPU instances
D. An AWS Health event subscription for EC2 performance issues
correct: B. A CloudWatch alarm on the CPUUtilization metric with an SNS action that notifies the on-call channel
explanation: CloudWatch collects EC2 metrics (including CPUUtilization) and evaluates alarm thresholds. When the threshold is breached, the alarm triggers an action — typically publishing to an SNS topic that sends email, SMS, or a webhook to an on-call tool. This is the standard observability pattern for AWS infrastructure alerting. AWS Config (A) tracks resource configuration changes, not performance metrics. Trusted Advisor (C) provides best-practice recommendations but does not page on-call. AWS Health (D) covers service disruptions affecting AWS infrastructure, not individual instance CPU metrics. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/AlarmThatSendsEmail.html
topic: CloudWatch metrics and alarms for AWS infrastructure observability and alerting

question: A security audit finds that an IAM user has been granted the AWS-managed 'AdministratorAccess' policy for a CI/CD pipeline. What is the correct remediation?
choices: A. Replace the IAM user with an IAM role and attach a custom policy granting only the specific actions the pipeline needs
B. Keep the IAM user but enable MFA to compensate for the broad permissions
C. Switch to using the root account credentials in the pipeline for more auditability
D. Move the pipeline to a separate AWS Region to isolate the blast radius
correct: A. Replace the IAM user with an IAM role and attach a custom policy granting only the specific actions the pipeline needs
explanation: Least-privilege principle requires that the CI/CD pipeline be granted only the permissions it actually uses. An IAM role is preferred over an IAM user for machine identities because roles issue temporary, auto-rotated credentials. A custom policy scoped to exactly the required actions (e.g., s3:PutObject, ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition) eliminates unnecessary privilege. Adding MFA (B) improves authentication but does not reduce the permission scope. Root credentials (C) are the broadest possible permissions and must never be used for automation. Moving to another Region (D) does not change the permission scope. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html
topic: IAM roles vs users vs policies and least-privilege access in CI/CD pipelines

question: An S3 bucket contains archived log files that are accessed once or twice per year for compliance audits. Which S3 storage class minimizes ongoing storage cost while still making retrieval possible within hours?
choices: A. S3 Standard — highest availability and fastest access
B. S3 Standard-IA — lower cost but millisecond retrieval
C. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — very low cost with retrieval times of minutes to hours
D. S3 Intelligent-Tiering — automatically moves objects between access tiers
correct: C. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval — very low cost with retrieval times of minutes to hours
explanation: S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly S3 Glacier) is designed for data accessed rarely, offering storage costs around $0.004/GB/month — roughly 10x cheaper than S3 Standard. Retrieval takes minutes to hours, which is acceptable for a compliance audit that is planned in advance. S3 Standard (A) is the most expensive and unnecessary for cold archive data. S3 Standard-IA (B) is cheaper than Standard but far more expensive than Glacier, and its millisecond access is overkill for once-a-year retrieval. Intelligent-Tiering (D) is efficient for unpredictable access patterns but has monitoring overhead costs that outweigh savings for predictably cold data. Source: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/storage-classes/
topic: S3 storage classes and lifecycle policies for archival and cost optimization

question: A CloudWatch alarm for high database connection count has been firing for three days but the team does not know which application component is causing the spike. Which CloudWatch capability helps isolate the source?
choices: A. CloudWatch Synthetics — runs canary scripts to simulate user traffic
B. CloudWatch Logs Insights — queries structured log data to identify which service or function is opening connections
C. CloudWatch Events — routes AWS API events to Lambda for remediation
D. CloudWatch Contributor Insights — analyzes log data to identify top contributors to a metric anomaly
correct: D. CloudWatch Contributor Insights — analyzes log data to identify top contributors to a metric anomaly
explanation: CloudWatch Contributor Insights is specifically designed to identify the 'top N contributors' to a pattern — for example, which IP addresses, user agents, or service names account for the most database connections. It continuously analyzes log data and surfaces the largest contributors to the observed spike without requiring manual query writing. Synthetics (A) tests endpoint availability, not connection analysis. Logs Insights (B) requires writing queries and is exploratory rather than automatic; it is useful but not the most targeted tool for this pattern. CloudWatch Events (now EventBridge) (C) routes API events, not metric contributors. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/ContributorInsights.html
topic: CloudWatch metrics, alarms, and Contributor Insights for identifying anomaly sources

question: A company needs to enforce that every EC2 instance across all AWS accounts in an organization has a 'CostCenter' tag. Which IAM/SCP mechanism enforces this at the organizational level?
choices: A. An IAM permission boundary attached to every developer role
B. An AWS Organizations Service Control Policy (SCP) that denies EC2 RunInstances if the CostCenter tag is absent
C. A CloudWatch alarm that detects untagged instances and sends an SNS alert
D. An AWS Config rule with auto-remediation that terminates untagged instances
correct: B. An AWS Organizations Service Control Policy (SCP) that denies EC2 RunInstances if the CostCenter tag is absent
explanation: Service Control Policies (SCPs) are applied at the AWS Organizations level and act as guardrails that restrict what actions can be taken in member accounts, regardless of the IAM permissions in those accounts. An SCP denying ec2:RunInstances when the required tag is absent prevents untagged instances from ever being created. Permission boundaries (A) limit a single IAM entity, not all users across all accounts. CloudWatch alarms (C) are detective controls — they alert after the fact but don't prevent the action. AWS Config (D) can auto-remediate but introduces a race window where untagged instances run briefly. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html
topic: IAM policies, SCPs, and organizational guardrails for enforcing governance across AWS accounts

question: A platform team is organizing resources for three product lines (Payments, Identity, Analytics) each with their own dev, staging, and production environments in Google Cloud. Which GCP resource hierarchy structure best represents this?
choices: A. One GCP project per product line; use namespaces within the project to separate environments
B. Organization → three Folders (one per product line) → multiple Projects within each folder (one per environment)
C. One GCP project for all product lines with labels to identify environment and product line
D. Organization → one shared Folder → all projects in the shared folder with IAM conditions
correct: B. Organization → three Folders (one per product line) → multiple Projects within each folder (one per environment)
explanation: GCP's resource hierarchy is: Organization → Folders → Projects → Resources. Folders represent organizational units (e.g., teams or product lines) and provide IAM and policy inheritance boundaries. Projects are the fundamental isolation unit for billing, APIs, and resources. Using a Folder per product line and a Project per environment provides clean IAM delegation (the Payments team's folder admin cannot touch Identity resources) and per-project billing granularity. Using one project for everything (A, C) blurs billing and IAM boundaries. A single shared folder (D) removes the product-line isolation that makes folder-per-team valuable. Source: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/cloud-platform-resource-hierarchy
topic: GCP resource hierarchy: organization, folders, and projects as isolation and policy boundaries

question: A team is running a Kubernetes-based microservices application and needs to choose between Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) and Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). Which statement most accurately describes a differentiating trade-off?
choices: A. GKE runs standard Kubernetes; AKS uses a proprietary container orchestrator incompatible with standard Kubernetes
B. GKE Autopilot offers a fully managed, node-abstracted Kubernetes experience; AKS requires the team to manage node pool OS upgrades manually by default
C. AKS integrates with Active Directory for RBAC; GKE has no RBAC capability
D. GKE is only available in US regions; AKS is globally available
correct: B. GKE Autopilot offers a fully managed, node-abstracted Kubernetes experience; AKS requires the team to manage node pool OS upgrades manually by default
explanation: GKE Autopilot is a distinctive managed Kubernetes mode where Google manages node provisioning, sizing, and OS patching — teams only deploy workloads. AKS in its standard mode requires node pool management including periodic OS image upgrades (though AKS also has a managed cluster upgrade path). Both GKE and AKS run upstream Kubernetes (A is false). Both support Kubernetes RBAC and cloud-provider identity integration (C is false — GKE integrates with Google Cloud IAM and Workload Identity). Both services are globally available (D is false). Source: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/autopilot-overview; https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/
topic: GKE vs AKS: managed Kubernetes trade-offs between Google Cloud and Azure

question: An Azure-based platform uses Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). A pod needs to read a secret from Azure Key Vault without storing credentials in the pod's environment variables or configuration files. Which mechanism supports this?
choices: A. Mount the Key Vault secret as a Kubernetes ConfigMap volume
B. Use Azure Managed Identity with the AKS workload identity feature to authenticate the pod to Key Vault without any credential
C. Inject the service principal client secret into the pod via a Kubernetes Secret object
D. Grant the AKS node pool's VM Scale Set identity access to Key Vault and use instance metadata service from the pod
correct: B. Use Azure Managed Identity with the AKS workload identity feature to authenticate the pod to Key Vault without any credential
explanation: Azure Workload Identity (successor to AAD Pod Identity) lets a Kubernetes service account federate with an Azure Managed Identity using OIDC token exchange. The pod receives a short-lived, automatically rotated token with no static credential stored anywhere. ConfigMap (A) stores plaintext data — secrets should never be in ConfigMaps. Injecting the client secret into a Kubernetes Secret (C) is better than env vars but still stores a long-lived credential. Node-level VM identity (D) grants that identity to ALL pods on the node, violating least-privilege. Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/workload-identity-overview
topic: Managed identity for workload authentication without credentials in Azure and AKS

question: A platform engineer needs to grant a junior developer read-only access to all resources in an Azure subscription without granting access to billing. Which Azure construct handles this?
choices: A. Add the developer to the Azure AD Global Administrator role
B. Assign the built-in 'Reader' RBAC role to the developer at the subscription scope
C. Create a subscription-level budget alert and add the developer as a recipient
D. Add the developer to the 'Billing Reader' role at the subscription scope
correct: B. Assign the built-in 'Reader' RBAC role to the developer at the subscription scope
explanation: Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) uses role assignments at a scope (management group, subscription, resource group, or resource). The built-in 'Reader' role grants read-only access to all resources in the scope without granting billing or administrative permissions. Global Administrator (A) is an Azure AD directory role granting tenant-wide admin rights — far beyond the requirement. A budget alert recipient (C) can view cost alerts but has no resource access. Billing Reader (D) grants access to billing data specifically, not to Azure resources. Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/overview
topic: Azure AD (Entra ID) RBAC and role assignments for least-privilege access control

question: A team deploying resources in Azure wants to group a web app, its database, and its storage account together so they can be managed, tagged, and deleted as a unit during testing. Which Azure construct enables this?
choices: A. An Azure Management Group containing one subscription per resource type
B. An Azure Resource Group containing all three resources
C. An Azure Availability Set spanning all three resources
D. An Azure Policy initiative applied to the subscription
correct: B. An Azure Resource Group containing all three resources
explanation: An Azure Resource Group is a logical container for related Azure resources that share the same lifecycle, permissions, and tags. Deleting the resource group deletes all resources inside it — ideal for test environments. Management Groups (A) are for organizing subscriptions, not individual resources. Availability Sets (C) group VMs for fault and update domain distribution — they have nothing to do with grouping disparate resource types. Azure Policy (D) enforces governance rules but does not group resources for lifecycle management. Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/manage-resource-groups-portal
topic: Azure resource groups as management containers for lifecycle, tagging, and access control

question: A GCP workload running on GKE needs to read objects from a Cloud Storage bucket without using service account key files. Which GCP mechanism enables this?
choices: A. Download the service account JSON key and mount it as a Kubernetes Secret
B. Use GKE Workload Identity to bind the Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account, granting it Cloud Storage permissions without any key file
C. Grant the default Compute Engine service account storage.admin on the bucket
D. Annotate the pod with the bucket name and let GCP auto-discover the credentials
correct: B. Use GKE Workload Identity to bind the Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account, granting it Cloud Storage permissions without any key file
explanation: GKE Workload Identity is the recommended way for GKE workloads to authenticate to GCP APIs. It maps a Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account using OIDC federation. The pod's calls to Cloud Storage are authenticated via a short-lived token with no key file stored anywhere. Mounting a JSON key (A) creates a long-lived, broadly scoped credential that can be stolen from the pod filesystem. Granting the default Compute Engine service account (C) grants that access to ALL pods on ALL nodes in the cluster, violating least-privilege. GCP does not auto-discover credentials from pod annotations (D). Source: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/workload-identity
topic: Managed identity and workload identity for GKE authentication without credential files

question: A company uses Azure Blob Storage for unstructured data and Google Cloud Storage for analytics pipelines. Which statement most accurately describes an operational difference between the two?
choices: A. Azure Blob Storage uses a globally flat namespace; GCS uses hierarchically structured buckets that prevent name conflicts

B. GCS uses a flat-namespace bucket model like Azure Blob Storage, but GCS storage classes (Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive) map directly to cost-vs-retrieval trade-offs similar to Azure's Hot, Cool, and Archive tiers
C. Azure Blob Storage supports object versioning; GCS does not
D. GCS buckets are Region-scoped only; Azure Blob Storage supports multi-region replication natively
correct: B. GCS uses a flat-namespace bucket model like Azure Blob Storage, but GCS storage classes (Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive) map directly to cost-vs-retrieval trade-offs similar to Azure's Hot, Cool, and Archive tiers
explanation: Both GCS and Azure Blob Storage use flat-namespace object stores (apparent folder structures are naming conventions, not true directories). Both offer storage class tiers that trade access frequency for storage cost: GCS (Standard, Nearline 30-day min, Coldline 90-day min, Archive 365-day min) parallel Azure Blob's Hot, Cool, Cold, and Archive tiers. Azure Blob Storage is not globally flat-namespaced in isolation from account names (A is incorrect framing). Both services support object versioning (C is false). GCS supports multi-region and dual-region buckets; Azure also offers geo-redundant replication (D is false). Source: https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes; https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/access-tiers-overview
topic: Google Cloud Storage vs Azure Blob Storage: storage classes and operational concepts

question: A company's Azure environment uses Azure Active Directory (now Entra ID) for identity. A platform engineer wants to ensure only members of the 'platform-eng' security group can deploy to the production subscription. Which is the correct approach?
choices: A. Set a conditional access policy requiring the group to use MFA when deploying, which also restricts non-members
B. Assign the Contributor RBAC role on the production subscription to only the 'platform-eng' Azure AD group
C. Configure a resource lock on the production subscription to block all write operations
D. Enable Azure Defender for the subscription, which restricts unauthorized deployments
correct: B. Assign the Contributor RBAC role on the production subscription to only the 'platform-eng' Azure AD group
explanation: Azure RBAC role assignments are the correct mechanism for authorization. By assigning 'Contributor' (or a custom role scoped to deployment actions) to the 'platform-eng' Azure AD group at the subscription scope, only group members can deploy resources. Non-members lack the role and are denied. Conditional access (A) enforces authentication policy (e.g., MFA) but does not control authorization — a non-member with MFA would still be denied by the role assignment, and MFA alone does not restrict resource deployment. Resource locks (C) block delete/modify operations for everyone, including platform-eng. Azure Defender (D) is a threat detection service, not an authorization control. Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/role-assignments-portal
topic: Azure AD (Entra ID) and RBAC for controlling deployment authorization

question: In GCP's resource hierarchy, at which level are billing accounts attached, and why does this matter for a multi-team organization?
choices: A. Billing accounts attach at the Folder level, allowing per-team billing aggregation
B. Billing accounts attach at the Project level, meaning each project's costs are independently tracked and can be allocated to separate billing accounts for different teams or business units
C. Billing accounts attach at the Organization level only, so the entire organization shares a single invoice
D. Billing accounts attach at the Resource level, enabling per-resource cost attribution
correct: B. Billing accounts attach at the Project level, meaning each project's costs are independently tracked and can be allocated to separate billing accounts for different teams or business units
explanation: In GCP, every project is linked to exactly one Cloud Billing account, and all resource usage within the project is charged to that billing account. This makes the Project the unit of cost allocation. A multi-team organization can link each team's projects to a separate billing account, enabling independent invoicing, budget alerts, and quota management per team. Billing does not attach to folders (A) — folders are IAM and policy containers. While costs roll up to the organization level for reporting, multiple billing accounts can exist within one organization (C is an oversimplification). Individual resources do not have their own billing account attachments (D). Source: https://cloud.google.com/billing/docs/concepts
topic: GCP resource hierarchy: organization, folders, and projects and their relationship to billing

question: A GKE workload needs to access the Google Cloud SQL API. The platform engineer grants the 'roles/cloudsql.client' IAM role to the GCP service account linked to the pod via Workload Identity. The pod still gets 'permission denied' errors. What is the most likely missing step?
choices: A. The GKE cluster must be upgraded to the latest version before Workload Identity takes effect
B. The Kubernetes service account annotation binding it to the GCP service account is missing or incorrect
C. The Cloud SQL instance must be in the same GCP project as the GKE cluster
D. The pod must restart the GKE node pool to pick up the new IAM binding
correct: B. The Kubernetes service account annotation binding it to the GCP service account is missing or incorrect
explanation: GKE Workload Identity requires two bindings: (1) an IAM binding allowing the Kubernetes service account to impersonate the GCP service account (iam.workloadIdentityUser role), and (2) a Kubernetes service account annotation specifying the GCP service account (iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account). If either binding is missing or has a typo, the pod receives no GCP credentials and gets 'permission denied'. Upgrading the cluster (A) is not related to a misconfigured binding. Cloud SQL and GKE can be in different projects as long as IAM is correctly configured across them (C is false). Restarting the node pool (D) is not required — Workload Identity changes take effect at pod restart, not node restart. Source: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity
topic: GKE Workload Identity binding troubleshooting and configuration requirements

question: A company has three AWS VPCs (Production, Staging, Development) and needs all three to communicate with each other and with a shared services VPC (DNS, monitoring). They also expect to add more VPCs in the future. Which connectivity model scales best?
choices: A. Create VPC peering connections between every pair of VPCs in a full mesh
B. Use AWS Transit Gateway as a central hub; attach all VPCs as spokes
C. Use AWS PrivateLink to expose services from each VPC to the others
D. Merge all VPCs into a single VPC with separate subnets for each environment
correct: B. Use AWS Transit Gateway as a central hub; attach all VPCs as spokes
explanation: Transit Gateway implements a hub-and-spoke model where each VPC is attached to the TGW, which routes traffic between them. Adding a new VPC requires only one new TGW attachment, not N-1 new peering connections. VPC peering (A) is a full mesh: with N VPCs, you need N*(N-1)/2 peering connections — 4 VPCs would need 6 connections, and 10 VPCs would need 45. Peering also does not support transitive routing, so VPCs cannot route through an intermediary. PrivateLink (C) exposes specific services (not general routing) via endpoint interfaces — useful for service exposure, not general inter-VPC routing. Merging into one VPC (D) eliminates the isolation that separate VPCs provide. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/tgw/what-is-transit-gateway.html
topic: VPC peering vs Transit Gateway for scalable inter-VPC connectivity in AWS

question: An internal microservice running in a VPC needs to be consumed by other teams' services also running in VPCs, but the platform team does not want to expose it to the internet or require VPC peering. Which AWS service is designed for this use case?
choices: A. AWS Direct Connect — dedicated private connectivity from on-premises to AWS
B. AWS PrivateLink — allows services to be consumed across VPCs via private endpoint interfaces without peering or internet exposure
C. AWS Transit Gateway — routes traffic between VPCs at the network layer
D. An Application Load Balancer with an internet-facing scheme and IP allowlisting
correct: B. AWS PrivateLink — allows services to be consumed across VPCs via private endpoint interfaces without peering or internet exposure
explanation: AWS PrivateLink enables service providers to expose a Network Load Balancer-backed service as a VPC Endpoint Service. Consumers in other VPCs create an Interface VPC Endpoint to reach it privately over AWS's internal network — no internet, no peering, no overlapping CIDR concerns. Direct Connect (A) connects on-premises data centers to AWS, not VPC-to-VPC communication. Transit Gateway (C) routes at the network layer and requires IP routing, which creates CIDR overlap risks and routes all traffic rather than exposing specific services. An internet-facing ALB (D) exposes the service to the internet, which the requirement explicitly prohibits. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/what-is-privatelink.html
topic: AWS PrivateLink for private service access across VPCs without internet exposure

question: A security engineer notices that an EC2 instance in a public subnet has been responding to unexpected port scans. They want to block traffic from a specific CIDR block at the subnet boundary. Which control is appropriate and why?
choices: A. Update the EC2 instance's Security Group to deny inbound traffic from the CIDR block
B. Add a DENY rule to the subnet's Network ACL (NACL) for the specific CIDR block
C. Detach the internet gateway from the VPC to block all inbound traffic
D. Enable AWS Shield Standard to block the CIDR block automatically
correct: B. Add a DENY rule to the subnet's Network ACL (NACL) for the specific CIDR block
explanation: NACLs are stateless, subnet-level firewall rules evaluated in numeric order. They support both ALLOW and DENY rules, making them the correct control for blocking a specific CIDR block before traffic reaches any instance in the subnet. Security Groups (A) are stateful and instance-level, but critically they support only ALLOW rules — you cannot add an explicit DENY in a Security Group. Detaching the internet gateway (C) blocks all inbound and outbound internet traffic, which is far too broad. AWS Shield Standard (D) provides DDoS protection but does not allow custom CIDR-based blocking. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html
topic: Security Groups vs NACLs: stateful vs stateless firewalls at instance and subnet level

question: A team's web application is being hit by a SQL injection attack through the HTTP query string. The application sits behind an Application Load Balancer. Which AWS service blocks this at the edge before it reaches the application?
choices: A. AWS Shield Advanced — protects against volumetric DDoS attacks
B. AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) — inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and blocks requests matching SQL injection patterns
C. AWS Network Firewall — provides stateful inspection at the VPC level
D. Amazon GuardDuty — detects threats in CloudTrail and VPC Flow Logs
correct: B. AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) — inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and blocks requests matching SQL injection patterns
explanation: AWS WAF operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and can be attached directly to an Application Load Balancer, CloudFront distribution, or API Gateway. It evaluates request content — including headers, query strings, and body — against managed or custom rule groups. AWS-managed WAF rule groups include SQL injection and XSS pattern matching. Shield Advanced (A) defends against volumetric DDoS at Layer 3/4 — it cannot inspect or block Layer 7 SQL injection patterns. Network Firewall (C) operates at the VPC network layer and is not application-aware. GuardDuty (D) analyzes logs after the fact for threat detection — it does not block in-flight requests. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-waf.html
topic: WAF placement and purpose for Layer 7 application attack protection

question: A security team wants continuous, automated threat detection across all AWS accounts in their organization — covering EC2 network traffic, CloudTrail API calls, and DNS queries — without deploying any agents. Which service provides this?
choices: A. AWS Inspector — scans EC2 instances for software vulnerabilities
B. AWS Config — records configuration changes and evaluates compliance rules
C. Amazon GuardDuty — analyzes VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail events, and DNS logs to detect threats using ML and threat intelligence
D. AWS Security Hub — aggregates findings from multiple security services into a single dashboard
correct: C. Amazon GuardDuty — analyzes VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail events, and DNS logs to detect threats using ML and threat intelligence
explanation: Amazon GuardDuty is an agentless threat detection service that continuously analyzes VPC Flow Logs, AWS CloudTrail management and data events, and Route 53 DNS query logs. It applies ML models and AWS threat intelligence to identify suspicious behaviors such as unusual API calls, communication with known malicious IPs, or credential exfiltration patterns. Inspector (A) performs vulnerability assessments on EC2 and Lambda — it is a vulnerability scanner, not a behavioral threat detector. AWS Config (B) tracks configuration state and compliance, not behavioral threats. Security Hub (D) aggregates and normalizes findings from GuardDuty and other services but does not generate its own threat detections from raw logs. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/what-is-guardduty.html
topic: GuardDuty and Microsoft Defender for agentless cloud threat detection

question: A platform engineer needs to encrypt an EBS volume at rest and wants the organization to control the key material so they can revoke access if needed. Which AWS KMS configuration achieves this?
choices: A. Use the AWS-managed key (aws/ebs) — AWS controls the key, and the customer controls when it is rotated
B. Create a Customer-Managed Key (CMK) in KMS, attach a key policy granting the organization admin rights, and use this CMK to encrypt the EBS volume
C. Use server-side encryption with a customer-provided key (SSE-C) stored on-premises
D. Enable EBS default encryption with the default AWS-owned key for maximum customer control
correct: B. Create a Customer-Managed Key (CMK) in KMS, attach a key policy granting the organization admin rights, and use this CMK to encrypt the EBS volume
explanation: Customer-Managed Keys (CMKs) in AWS KMS give the customer full control: they define the key policy, can restrict which principals can use the key, enable or disable the key, schedule deletion, and require custom audit trails via CloudTrail. Revoking access by disabling or deleting the CMK immediately prevents decryption of data protected by it. AWS-managed keys (A) are controlled by AWS — the customer cannot disable them or revoke access independently. SSE-C (C) applies to S3, not EBS, and requires the customer to supply the key on every API call. AWS-owned keys (D) are the least configurable — customers have no visibility or control over them. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/concepts.html
topic: KMS key types and customer-managed keys for encryption at rest with revocability

question: A Security Group rule allows inbound TCP port 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 to an EC2 instance. A NACL on the same subnet has a DENY rule for port 443 from a specific IP (numbered 90) and an ALLOW rule for port 443 from 0.0.0.0/0 (numbered 100). Traffic from the specific IP still reaches the instance. What explains this behavior?
choices: A. Security Groups take precedence over NACLs, so the Security Group ALLOW overrides the NACL DENY

B. NACLs evaluate rules in numeric order starting from the lowest number; rule 90 (DENY) is evaluated before rule 100 (ALLOW), so traffic from that IP should be denied — the engineer should verify the DENY rule is correctly applied to the right subnet and IP
C. NACLs only apply to outbound traffic; inbound traffic is governed solely by Security Groups
D. The DENY rule requires a corresponding inbound AND outbound rule to be effective because NACLs are stateful
correct: B. NACLs evaluate rules in numeric order starting from the lowest number; rule 90 (DENY) is evaluated before rule 100 (ALLOW), so traffic from that IP should be denied — the engineer should verify the DENY rule is correctly applied to the right subnet and IP
explanation: NACLs process rules in ascending numeric order and stop at the first matching rule. Rule 90 (DENY for the specific IP) has a lower number than rule 100 (ALLOW all), so the DENY should be matched first for that IP. If traffic is still reaching the instance, the most likely cause is a configuration error: the NACL is applied to the wrong subnet, the specific IP in the rule is incorrect, or the NACL was not saved/associated properly. Security Groups do not override NACLs (A) — both are evaluated, and a packet must pass both. NACLs apply to both inbound and outbound traffic (C is false). NACLs are stateless (not stateful as stated in D), meaning return traffic requires a separate outbound rule, but this does not affect whether the DENY rule blocks inbound traffic. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html
topic: Security Groups vs NACLs: rule evaluation order and stateless vs stateful behavior

question: A company wants to connect its on-premises private network to AWS VPCs in multiple regions without traffic traversing the public internet. Which combination of services achieves this?
choices: A. AWS Direct Connect (dedicated private link to AWS) combined with Transit Gateway (routing between VPCs)
B. AWS Site-to-Site VPN over the internet combined with VPC peering
C. AWS PrivateLink endpoints deployed in each VPC, accessible from on-premises via the internet
D. Amazon CloudFront with Origin Shield to route on-premises traffic to regional VPCs
correct: A. AWS Direct Connect (dedicated private link to AWS) combined with Transit Gateway (routing between VPCs)
explanation: AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated private physical connection between the customer's data center and an AWS Direct Connect location — traffic never traverses the public internet. Transit Gateway can be attached to the Direct Connect Gateway, enabling the on-premises network to reach multiple VPCs across regions through a single connection. Site-to-Site VPN (B) encrypts traffic but routes it over the public internet, which the requirement prohibits. PrivateLink (C) exposes specific services, not general network routing, and would still require internet access from on-premises. CloudFront (D) is a CDN — it is not a network connectivity solution for on-premises-to-VPC routing. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html
topic: AWS Direct Connect and Transit Gateway for private hybrid connectivity

question: Microsoft Defender for Cloud reports a 'high severity' finding: a storage account in Azure has public blob access enabled and contains sensitive data. Which is the correct immediate remediation?
choices: A. Enable Azure DDoS Protection Standard on the storage account
B. Disable public blob access on the storage account and validate that authorized workloads use private endpoints or managed identity instead
C. Enable storage account encryption with a customer-managed key
D. Move the storage account to a different Azure region with stricter default network policies
correct: B. Disable public blob access on the storage account and validate that authorized workloads use private endpoints or managed identity instead
explanation: Public blob access allows anyone on the internet to read objects in the storage account without authentication — a critical misconfiguration for sensitive data. The correct remediation is to disable the 'Allow Blob Anonymous Access' setting on the storage account, then ensure authorized workloads authenticate via Managed Identity or access the account over a private endpoint. DDoS Protection (A) mitigates volumetric attacks but does not restrict unauthorized data access. Encryption (C) protects data confidentiality at rest but does not prevent public access — an unauthenticated caller can still download encrypted blobs if public access is enabled. Moving regions (D) does not change the public access configuration. Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/anonymous-read-access-prevent
topic: Microsoft Defender for Cloud findings and Azure storage security remediation

question: A platform team needs to ensure that all data written to an RDS database is encrypted at rest, and that they can audit every use of the encryption key. Which configuration achieves both requirements?
choices: A. Enable RDS encryption using an AWS-managed key; RDS encryption is on by default
B. Enable RDS encryption at rest using a Customer-Managed KMS Key (CMK); all KMS API calls are automatically logged to CloudTrail
C. Enable RDS encryption and configure a CloudWatch metric filter to count KMS calls
D. Enable RDS encryption with an AWS-owned key and enable RDS Performance Insights for key usage visibility
correct: B. Enable RDS encryption at rest using a Customer-Managed KMS Key (CMK); all KMS API calls are automatically logged to CloudTrail
explanation: Enabling RDS encryption with a CMK satisfies both requirements: data is encrypted at rest, and every KMS API call (GenerateDataKey, Decrypt, etc.) is automatically recorded in AWS CloudTrail, providing a complete, auditable history of key usage. AWS-managed keys (A) encrypt data but the customer cannot control key policies or easily revoke access. A CloudWatch metric filter (C) is a manual, partial solution; CloudTrail is the native, complete audit mechanism for KMS. AWS-owned keys (D) provide the least customer visibility — the customer cannot see key usage events in CloudTrail. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/Overview.Encryption.html
topic: KMS customer-managed keys and CloudTrail for encryption at rest with full key usage auditing

question: A service has redundant instances in two Availability Zones behind a load balancer. When one AZ goes down, the other AZ continues serving traffic. What does this architecture achieve?
choices: A. Fault tolerance — the system survives a complete AZ failure with zero downtime
B. High availability — the system reduces the impact of failures and recovers quickly, but does not guarantee zero downtime during failover
C. Disaster recovery — the system automatically restores from the last backup when an AZ fails
D. Geo-redundancy — the system distributes traffic across multiple geographic regions
correct: B. High availability — the system reduces the impact of failures and recovers quickly, but does not guarantee zero downtime during failover
explanation: High availability (HA) is achieved by deploying redundant components so that the system continues operating when individual components fail. Multi-AZ with a load balancer achieves HA: if one AZ fails, the load balancer routes traffic to the healthy AZ with minimal disruption. However, there may be brief in-flight request failures during failover. Fault tolerance (A) is a stronger guarantee — it means the system continues with zero visible impact even during complete component failure, typically requiring active-active redundancy with stateful session sharing. Disaster recovery (C) refers to restoring a system after a major event, not real-time failover. Geo-redundancy (D) requires multiple Regions, not multiple AZs within one Region. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar.
topic: High availability vs fault tolerance: distinguishing redundancy levels and their guarantees

question: A company's e-commerce platform must continue processing orders even if an entire AWS Region becomes unavailable. Which architecture pattern achieves this?
choices: A. Multi-AZ deployment within a single Region with an RDS Multi-AZ standby
B. Multi-Region active-active deployment with global load balancing routing customers to the nearest healthy Region
C. Multi-Region active-passive deployment where traffic only goes to the passive Region when the primary fails
D. Single-Region deployment with enhanced EC2 instance types for maximum reliability
correct: B. Multi-Region active-active deployment with global load balancing routing customers to the nearest healthy Region
explanation: Active-active multi-Region means both (or all) Regions serve live traffic simultaneously. If one Region fails, the global load balancer (e.g., AWS Route 53 with latency or health-check routing) automatically directs all traffic to the remaining healthy Regions with no manual intervention. Multi-AZ (A) protects against data center failures within a Region but cannot survive a full Regional outage. Active-passive multi-Region (C) requires a failover event where traffic shifts from primary to passive — during failover, orders may be lost or delayed; additionally, the passive Region must be warmed up. A single-Region deployment (D), regardless of instance size, cannot survive a Regional AWS outage. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar (Multi-Region Resiliency).
topic: Multi-region active-active vs active-passive architectures for regional failure resilience

question: A company's disaster recovery plan states: 'We can tolerate losing up to 4 hours of transactions, and the system must be operational within 1 hour of declaring a disaster.' Which metrics map to these tolerances?
choices: A. RTO = 4 hours (transaction loss), RPO = 1 hour (recovery time)
B. RPO = 4 hours (data loss tolerance), RTO = 1 hour (recovery time tolerance)
C. SLA = 4 hours downtime per year, MTTR = 1 hour per incident
D. RPO = 1 hour (data loss tolerance), RTO = 4 hours (recovery time tolerance)
correct: B. RPO = 4 hours (data loss tolerance), RTO = 1 hour (recovery time tolerance)
explanation: Recovery Point Objective (RPO) is the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time — how old the most recent backup can be. '4 hours of transactions' means the company can tolerate losing up to 4 hours of data, so RPO = 4 hours. Recovery Time Objective (RTO) is the maximum acceptable time to restore service after a disaster is declared — '1 hour to be operational' means RTO = 1 hour. Option A reverses the definitions. Option C introduces SLA and MTTR, which measure availability and mean time to repair in normal operations, not disaster recovery tolerances. Option D also reverses the values. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar (Disaster Recovery).
topic: Disaster recovery metrics: RPO (data loss tolerance) and RTO (recovery time tolerance)

question: A team is comparing serverless (AWS Lambda) versus container-based (ECS/EKS) deployment for a new microservice that handles sporadic, unpredictable traffic spikes. Which serverless trade-off is most important to evaluate?
choices: A. Lambda cannot scale to more than 100 concurrent executions, making it unsuitable for spikes
B. Lambda has cold starts — the first invocation after a period of inactivity incurs initialization latency, which may be unacceptable for latency-sensitive requests
C. Lambda costs more than ECS at any traffic level due to per-invocation pricing
D. Lambda does not support environment variables, making configuration management complex
correct: B. Lambda has cold starts — the first invocation after a period of inactivity incurs initialization latency, which may be unacceptable for latency-sensitive requests
explanation: Cold starts are the primary operational trade-off for Lambda: when no warm execution environment exists (after periods of inactivity or during sudden scale-out), Lambda must initialize the runtime and load the function code, adding 100ms–several seconds of latency. For sporadic traffic, cold starts are frequent. Lambda scales to tens of thousands of concurrent executions (A is false — the default limit is 1,000 per account, but this is adjustable). Lambda is extremely cost-effective for sporadic workloads (C is incorrect — cost savings at low traffic are a primary benefit). Lambda fully supports environment variables (D is false). Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Performance Efficiency Pillar; https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-runtime-environment.html
topic: Serverless trade-offs: cold starts, scaling behavior, and cost vs container-based deployments

question: A platform team's cost report shows the production environment is spending $40,000/month on EC2 instances, but CloudWatch metrics show average CPU utilization at 8% and average memory at 12%. What is the most impactful cost optimization action?
choices: A. Purchase 3-year Reserved Instances for all current instance types to lock in the discount
B. Right-size the instances to a smaller type matching actual utilization, then evaluate Reserved Instance or Savings Plans commitment for the right-sized baseline
C. Enable EC2 Auto Scaling to scale to zero during off-peak hours
D. Migrate all instances to Spot to immediately cut costs by 70%
correct: B. Right-size the instances to a smaller type matching actual utilization, then evaluate Reserved Instance or Savings Plans commitment for the right-sized baseline
explanation: Right-sizing should always precede any commitment purchase. Locking in a 3-year Reserved Instance (A) at an oversized instance type would save 30-72% on wasted spend — still wasted spend. At 8% CPU and 12% memory, the instances are massively over-provisioned; reducing the instance type first captures the largest savings. Auto Scaling to zero (C) may not be appropriate for production workloads with availability SLAs, and production services typically need some minimum capacity. Migrating production to Spot (D) introduces interruption risk for services that cannot tolerate 2-minute preemption notices. The correct sequence is: right-size → commit. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cost Optimization Pillar.
topic: Cost optimization: right-sizing before commitment purchasing and auto-scaling strategies

question: An application currently serves all traffic from a single AWS Region (us-east-1). After a design review, the team decides to add a read replica in eu-west-1 and use Route 53 latency-based routing. What does this deployment achieve, and what limitation remains?
choices: A. This achieves active-active multi-Region; all writes and reads are handled globally with no limitations
B. This achieves active-passive multi-Region; all traffic fails over to eu-west-1 if us-east-1 is unavailable

C. This achieves reduced read latency for European users and some read redundancy, but write traffic still flows only to us-east-1 — a Regional failure of us-east-1 would still take down write capability
D. This achieves full fault tolerance — the RDS read replica automatically promotes to primary if us-east-1 fails
correct: C. This achieves reduced read latency for European users and some read redundancy, but write traffic still flows only to us-east-1 — a Regional failure of us-east-1 would still take down write capability
explanation: A read replica in eu-west-1 with latency-based routing improves read performance for European users and can continue serving reads if us-east-1 fails. However, a read replica is read-only by definition — writes must still go to the primary in us-east-1. A Regional failure of us-east-1 would halt writes (and potentially reads of fresh data). This is not active-active (A) — active-active requires write capability in all Regions. Active-passive (B) specifically means one Region is passive/standby and takes over only on failure — this scenario serves reads actively from eu-west-1 at all times. Read replicas do not automatically promote to primary cross-Region (D) — promotion requires manual intervention or an automated cross-Region failover implementation. Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar.
topic: Multi-region active-active vs active-passive trade-offs and read replica limitations

question: A team is designing a disaster recovery strategy for a critical database. They want an RPO of 15 minutes. Which backup approach satisfies this?
choices: A. Daily automated RDS snapshots retained for 7 days
B. RDS automated backups with continuous transaction log shipping to S3, enabling point-in-time recovery to any second within the retention window
C. Weekly manual snapshots with cross-Region copy enabled
D. RDS Multi-AZ standby — the standby is a hot replica that serves as the backup
correct: B. RDS automated backups with continuous transaction log shipping to S3, enabling point-in-time recovery to any second within the retention window
explanation: An RPO of 15 minutes means the most recent recoverable state cannot be older than 15 minutes. RDS automated backups continuously ship transaction logs to S3 alongside daily snapshots, enabling point-in-time recovery (PITR) to any second within the backup retention window (up to 35 days). In practice, this gives an RPO of approximately 5 minutes. Daily snapshots (A) give an RPO of up to 24 hours — far worse than 15 minutes. Weekly snapshots (C) give an RPO of up to 7 days. RDS Multi-AZ (D) is a high availability feature that enables fast failover within a Region; the standby replica is not a backup and cannot be used for point-in-time restoration from a logical error (e.g., accidental DELETE). Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_PIT.html
topic: Disaster recovery RPO and RTO: point-in-time recovery vs snapshot backup strategies

question: A Lambda function processes images uploaded to S3. At low traffic (10 images/hour), Lambda is very cost-effective. Traffic grows to 5 million images/hour sustained 24/7. Which cost concern becomes relevant at this scale?
choices: A. Lambda cannot process more than 1 million invocations per day
B. At sustained high volume, Lambda's per-invocation and per-GB-second pricing can exceed the cost of a right-sized container fleet running continuously on ECS or EKS
C. Lambda charges a flat monthly fee per function that grows with invocation count
D. Lambda's 15-minute maximum execution timeout prevents processing large images at high volume
correct: B. At sustained high volume, Lambda's per-invocation and per-GB-second pricing can exceed the cost of a right-sized container fleet running continuously on ECS or EKS
explanation: Serverless economics invert at scale: Lambda charges per invocation plus duration (GB-seconds). For sporadic workloads, this is extremely efficient because you pay only when invoked. But for sustained, high-throughput workloads, a continuously running container fleet on ECS/EKS may be cheaper because those instances serve many requests without per-invocation overhead. This is a genuine serverless trade-off documented by Netflix, Cloudflare, and others who moved back to containers at scale. Lambda's concurrency limit is configurable above 1,000 per account (A is false). Lambda uses pay-per-use pricing, not a flat monthly fee (C is false). Image processing in under 15 minutes per image is generally feasible; the throughput is handled by concurrent invocations, not longer execution (D is misleading). Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Cost Optimization Pillar.
topic: Serverless trade-offs: cost at scale compared to container-based alternatives

question: A team wants to implement auto-scaling for an ECS service to reduce costs during overnight low-traffic periods. Which metric and scaling policy combination is most appropriate for a web service?
choices: A. Scale on EC2 CPUUtilization using a simple scaling policy with a fixed step size
B. Scale on the Application Load Balancer RequestCountPerTarget metric using target tracking scaling to maintain a defined requests-per-task threshold
C. Scale on DynamoDB consumed read capacity units to predict web tier demand
D. Scale on SQS queue depth using a step scaling policy when the queue exceeds 100 messages
correct: B. Scale on the Application Load Balancer RequestCountPerTarget metric using target tracking scaling to maintain a defined requests-per-task threshold
explanation: For a web service, the most direct demand signal is incoming request rate. ALB's RequestCountPerTarget metric measures how many requests each ECS task is handling. Target tracking scaling adjusts the task count automatically to keep this metric at the target value — adding tasks when each task exceeds the threshold, removing tasks when under-utilized. This is more responsive and accurate than EC2 CPU (A), which lags behind actual request volume and is the host-level metric, not a per-task web metric. DynamoDB consumed capacity (C) is a backend database metric, not a web tier demand signal. SQS queue depth (D) is appropriate for queue consumer scaling patterns, not for a synchronous web service behind a load balancer. Source: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/service-auto-scaling.html
topic: Auto-scaling strategies and cost optimization: right metrics for web service scaling policies

question: A team is choosing between active-active and active-passive multi-Region deployment for a global payment service. Which statement most accurately captures the key trade-off?
choices: A. Active-active is always better; active-passive is only used for legacy systems

B. Active-active provides lower RTO and serves global users with lower latency, but requires distributed data consistency solutions and significantly more operational complexity; active-passive is simpler but has higher RTO and idle capacity cost
C. Active-passive provides lower RTO because both Regions are always handling traffic and can instantly absorb a failure
D. Active-active and active-passive have the same RTO because both require DNS failover
correct: B. Active-active provides lower RTO and serves global users with lower latency, but requires distributed data consistency solutions and significantly more operational complexity; active-passive is simpler but has higher RTO and idle capacity cost
explanation: Active-active multi-Region: both Regions serve live traffic, so if one fails, the other absorbs load immediately — RTO approaches zero. However, writes happen in multiple Regions simultaneously, requiring distributed consensus, conflict resolution, and eventual consistency management (e.g., DynamoDB Global Tables, CockroachDB). Active-passive: the passive Region is a standby that only activates on failover — simpler data model (single write region) but RTO includes DNS propagation time, passive Region warm-up, and data replication lag. The passive Region also runs continuously (or must be warmed up) at cost without serving traffic. Neither option is universally better (A is false). Active-passive has higher RTO, not lower (C reverses it). Both require DNS changes on failover, but active-active failover is instantaneous while passive failover involves TTL propagation delays (D is false). Source: AWS Well-Architected Framework — Reliability Pillar (Disaster Recovery Strategies).
topic: Multi-region active-active vs active-passive trade-offs for global payment services
